notes: Times are UTC. Resolution sources are public, machine-auditable feeds. close_time is when positions lock; resolution_time is the earliest the source data is reliably available.

markets.title: Lyrid meteor shower peak ZHR ≥18 on night of April 21/22, 2026
markets.resolution_source: International Meteor Organization (IMO) live ZHR graph at imo.net/members/imo_live_shower
markets.YES_condition: IMO live ZHR (corrected, 1-hour bin) for shower code LYR reaches 18 or higher in any bin between 2026-04-21 18:00Z and 2026-04-22 06:00Z.
markets.why_interesting: The Lyrids are the first big shower after winter — every amateur astronomer in the Northern Hemisphere has it circled, and the predicted peak rate is right at the threshold.
markets.ui_description: The Lyrids are the oldest recorded meteor shower — Chinese astronomers logged them in 687 BC. The 2026 peak falls on a moonless April night, making conditions favorable, but predicted ZHR of 18 sits right at the YES line. Trade based on weather over Europe, IMO live counts trickling in overnight, and whether anyone catches an outburst (Lyrids have surprised observers before, with 1982 producing rates of 90).

markets.title: Eta Aquariid meteor shower peak ZHR ≥40 on night of May 5/6, 2026
markets.resolution_source: International Meteor Organization (IMO) live ZHR graph at imo.net/members/imo_live_shower
markets.YES_condition: IMO live ZHR (corrected, 1-hour bin) for shower code ETA reaches 40 or higher in any bin between 2026-05-05 18:00Z and 2026-05-06 12:00Z.
markets.why_interesting: Halley's Comet debris stream, best from low latitudes — but a waning gibbous moon on May 1 means moonlight will wash out fainter meteors. Will rates still cross the threshold?
markets.ui_description: Every May, Earth plows through the dust trail left by Halley's Comet, and the southern hemisphere gets a front-row seat. The 2026 shower peaks in the predawn hours of May 6, but a bright waning moon will drown out fainter trails. Typical ZHR is 40-60; the question is whether moonlight holds reported rates below 40 or whether bright Eta Aquariid fireballs punch through anyway.

markets.title: SpaceX Starlink launch from Vandenberg SLC-4E succeeds at ~02:00 UTC on April 22, 2026
markets.resolution_source: SpaceX official mission page (spacex.com/launches) and live webcast confirmation of payload deploy.
markets.YES_condition: Falcon 9 booster B1100 lifts off from Vandenberg SLC-4E within the launch window declared by SpaceX on April 22 UTC AND payload separation is confirmed during the webcast.
markets.why_interesting: Stellar's first day of trading lines up with a real California launch — a clean, fast settlement that shows the protocol resolves on real-world events within hours.
markets.ui_description: The first market on the Stellar protocol resolves the same night it opens. SpaceX is targeting an early-morning Vandenberg launch with booster B1100. Weather scrubs, range conflicts, or a last-second hold push this NO; a clean liftoff and second-stage payload deploy push it YES. Watch the SpaceX webcast — settlement happens minutes after MECO.

markets.title: ≥10 successful Falcon family launches between April 22 and May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: SpaceX official launch log (spacex.com/launches) cross-checked against the Wikipedia 'List of Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy launches' table.
markets.YES_condition: 10 or more Falcon 9 / Falcon Heavy missions reach successful payload deployment with liftoff timestamps between 2026-04-22 00:00Z and 2026-05-11 23:59Z (inclusive).
markets.why_interesting: SpaceX has been pushing toward 200+ launches this year. A 20-day window with 10 successes is a real cadence test — judges can watch the counter tick up.
markets.ui_description: SpaceX is averaging better than one launch every two days in 2026, and this market asks whether they sustain that pace across the Stellar judging window. Each successful Falcon mission moves the on-chain counter; weather scrubs, anomalies, or range conflicts can stall it. A standing live ticker on the market page makes this the most narratively compelling cadence bet during the hackathon.

markets.title: At least one M-class or stronger solar flare during judging week (April 25 – May 3, 2026)
markets.resolution_source: NOAA Space Weather Prediction Center GOES X-ray flux event list at services.swpc.noaa.gov/json/goes/primary/xray-flares-7-day.json
markets.YES_condition: At least one solar flare classified M1.0 or stronger appears in the NOAA SWPC GOES X-ray flare event list with peak time between 2026-04-25 00:00Z and 2026-05-03 23:59Z.
markets.why_interesting: Cycle 25 is past its peak but the declining phase is historically when the biggest flares fire. Anyone watching SWPC daily knows what M-class odds feel like.
markets.ui_description: Solar Cycle 25 is in its declining phase, but history shows the most violent flares often come after maximum. NOAA SWPC publishes the GOES X-ray feed in near real time, so this market resolves automatically off public data. Active region count, sunspot complexity, and any incoming Earth-facing rotation all move the price across nine days of trading.

markets.title: Geomagnetic storm reaches Kp ≥5 (G1+) at any point during judging week (April 25 – May 3, 2026)
markets.resolution_source: NOAA SWPC planetary K-index 3-hour estimates at services.swpc.noaa.gov/json/planetary_k_index_1m.json
markets.YES_condition: Estimated planetary Kp value reaches 5.00 or higher in any 3-hour bin with start time between 2026-04-25 00:00Z and 2026-05-03 21:00Z.
markets.why_interesting: G1 storms push aurora down to mid-latitudes — Scotland, Canada, even Tbilisi at extreme G3+. Aurora hunters will trade this one based on every CME alert.
markets.ui_description: A Kp index of 5 is the threshold where aurora starts pushing south of its usual oval, lighting up skies in Scotland, Scandinavia, and the northern US. Each CME launch off the Sun gets watched for Earth-direction; arrival times are 2-4 days later. Trade on coronagraph imagery from SOHO, ACE solar wind data, and the daily NOAA forecast.

markets.title: At least one X-class solar flare during April 22 – May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: NOAA Space Weather Prediction Center GOES X-ray flare event list at services.swpc.noaa.gov/json/goes/primary/xray-flares-7-day.json
markets.YES_condition: At least one solar flare classified X1.0 or stronger appears in NOAA SWPC's archived GOES X-ray flare events with peak time between 2026-04-22 00:00Z and 2026-05-11 23:59Z.
markets.why_interesting: X-class flares are the rarest and most spectacular — the ones that knock out HF radio and get TV news coverage. A 20-day window gives roughly fair odds in late Cycle 25.
markets.ui_description: X-class is the top tier — flares that release the energy of a billion hydrogen bombs and trigger radio blackouts on Earth's sunlit side. Cycle 25 produced an X9.0 in late 2024, and the declining phase historically hosts the biggest events. This longer-window market lets traders ride sunspot complexity changes for three weeks.

markets.title: Tbilisi daily maximum temperature ≥22°C on April 27, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for station nearest 41.7151°N, 44.8271°E, daily 'temperature_2m_max' field.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports temperature_2m_max for Tbilisi (lat 41.7151, lon 44.8271) on date 2026-04-27 as ≥22.0 °C.
markets.why_interesting: Late April in Tbilisi straddles the spring transition; the climatological average high is 18.8°C. 22°C is the line where locals start trading jackets for t-shirts.
markets.ui_description: Tbilisi springs are famously moody — one day jacket weather, the next a heatwave warning. The April climatological average max is 18.8°C, so 22°C requires a noticeable warm push. Open-Meteo's gridded reanalysis is the resolution source, available roughly 24 hours after midnight local. Traders can watch ECMWF and GFS forecasts converge across the week.

markets.title: Tbilisi total precipitation ≥5mm on May 1, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for station nearest 41.7151°N, 44.8271°E, daily 'precipitation_sum' field.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports precipitation_sum for Tbilisi (lat 41.7151, lon 44.8271) on date 2026-05-01 as ≥5.0 mm.
markets.why_interesting: May Day is a public holiday — everyone has outdoor plans. April averages 1.5mm/day across the month, so 5mm on a single day is a meaningful soak.
markets.ui_description: May 1 is a public holiday across Georgia and most of Europe, with picnics, concerts, and street markets all weather-dependent. Tbilisi sits at the edge of the Caucasus where Black Sea moisture meets continental air, and spring frontal systems are the main precipitation driver. Watch the 5-day GFS run — a clear front passing through can deliver 5mm in an hour.

markets.title: Stockholm overnight low ≤2°C on any night during April 25 – May 3, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for Stockholm-Arlanda station (59.6519°N, 17.9186°E), daily 'temperature_2m_min' field.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports temperature_2m_min ≤2.0 °C for Stockholm-Arlanda on at least one date between 2026-04-25 and 2026-05-03 inclusive.
markets.why_interesting: Northern Europe's late frosts are the bane of every gardener. Stockholm in late April typically sees lows of 2-5°C — right at the threshold, with Arctic plunges always possible.
markets.ui_description: Late April frost in Scandinavia decides whether tulips bloom on time and whether last weekend's planted vegetables survive. Stockholm-Arlanda is the standard reporting station, sitting on a flat plain that radiates heat fast on clear nights. Trade on ECMWF ensemble spread across the nine-day window — one clear, calm night anywhere is enough.

markets.title: London Heathrow records ≥1mm of rainfall on April 30, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for Heathrow (51.4700°N, -0.4543°E), daily 'precipitation_sum' field.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports precipitation_sum ≥1.0 mm for London Heathrow on date 2026-04-30.
markets.why_interesting: London in spring rains roughly half of all days. A 1mm threshold makes this a near-50/50 — perfect for liquidity provision and tight spreads.
markets.ui_description: London is famous for drizzle, and Heathrow's official rain gauge is the canonical UK reference. Climatologically, late April has a 45% daily chance of measurable rain, making this market a clean coin-flip — perfect for market makers and short-term traders. Watch UK Met Office shipping forecast and the position of any Atlantic low.

markets.title: Tbilisi cloud cover ≤30% during 22:00–04:00 local on night of May 5/6, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for Tbilisi (41.7151°N, 44.8271°E), hourly 'cloudcover' field, averaged over the six listed hours.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo hourly 'cloudcover' (in percent) averaged across hours 18:00, 19:00, 20:00, 21:00, 22:00, 23:00 UTC on 2026-05-05 (which span 22:00-03:00 local Tbilisi time) is ≤30.0%.
markets.why_interesting: This market exists precisely because of market sky-002 (Eta Aquariids). Tbilisi-based observers want to hedge their viewing-night cloud risk on-chain.
markets.ui_description: This is a hedge market — designed to pair naturally with the Eta Aquariid ZHR market. Tbilisi sits on the south slope of the Caucasus, where convective clouds often build in afternoons and clear at night, but spring frontal passages can ruin a viewing window. Trade based on ICON-EU and GFS cloud forecasts across the 24 hours leading up to close.

markets.title: Tbilisi records zero rainy days during judging week April 25 – May 1, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for Tbilisi (41.7151°N, 44.8271°E), daily 'precipitation_sum' field summed over 7 days.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports precipitation_sum <0.5 mm on every date from 2026-04-25 through 2026-05-01 inclusive (i.e., no day registers as a 'rainy day').
markets.why_interesting: April averages 16.8 rainy days in Tbilisi — a fully dry week is a tail outcome but not impossible. Long YES odds, big payout, perfect for degens.
markets.ui_description: A fully dry week in Tbilisi during late April is an unusual but not impossible event — the climatology says 16.8 rainy days per month, so any random 7-day window has a baseline NO probability around 90%. This is a long-tail YES market for traders who think a strong stable high will park over the Caucasus, and a steady NO bleed for those who believe the climatology.

markets.title: New York City (LaGuardia) daily high ≥25°C on May 7, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for LaGuardia (40.7769°N, -73.8740°E), daily 'temperature_2m_max' field.
markets.YES_condition: Open-Meteo reports temperature_2m_max ≥25.0 °C for LaGuardia Airport on date 2026-05-07.
markets.why_interesting: Early May is when NYC gets its first warm push — Central Park is in bloom, outdoor seating opens. 25°C / 77°F is the unofficial 'shorts weather' threshold.
markets.ui_description: Early May in New York is when patios open and Central Park hits peak bloom. LaGuardia's automated weather station is the standard local reference. Climatology says May 7 averages a high near 21°C, so 25°C requires a southerly flow — watch for setup of Atlantic high pressure off the Carolinas pushing warm air up the I-95 corridor.

markets.title: At least one M6.0+ earthquake anywhere in the world during judging week (April 25 – May 3, 2026)
markets.resolution_source: USGS Earthquake Catalog API at earthquake.usgs.gov/fdsnws/event/1/query (minmagnitude=6.0)
markets.YES_condition: USGS Earthquake Catalog returns at least one event with mag ≥6.0 and time between 2026-04-25 00:00:00 UTC and 2026-05-03 23:59:59 UTC.
markets.why_interesting: Globally, M6+ events average about one per week — so a 9-day window puts this around 75% YES. A near-certain YES creates an interesting liquidity-provision market.
markets.ui_description: M6.0+ earthquakes happen on Earth roughly once per week — about 130 per year. A 9-day judging window puts the baseline YES probability around 75%. This market is structured for liquidity providers and probability traders, with USGS publishing event data within minutes of detection. The price action will track real-time seismicity over Pacific subduction zones.

markets.title: M5.0+ earthquake within 300km of Tbilisi during April 22 – May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: USGS Earthquake Catalog API with circular search (latitude=41.7151, longitude=44.8271, maxradiuskm=300, minmagnitude=5.0).
markets.YES_condition: USGS Earthquake Catalog returns at least one event with mag ≥5.0 within 300 km of (41.7151, 44.8271) and origin time between 2026-04-22 00:00:00 UTC and 2026-05-11 23:59:59 UTC.
markets.why_interesting: The South Caucasus is seismically active — Turkish, Armenian, and Azerbaijani fault systems all sit within range. Locals feel something every few months.
markets.ui_description: The South Caucasus sits at the collision of the Arabian and Eurasian plates, with Turkey's North Anatolian Fault and Iran's Zagros system both within 300 km of Tbilisi. M5+ events in this region happen multiple times per year and are felt across the city. Resolves off the USGS public catalog within minutes of any qualifying event.

markets.title: Smithsonian Weekly Volcanic Activity Report adds at least one new ongoing eruption between April 22 and May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program Weekly Volcanic Activity Report at volcano.si.edu/news/WeeklyVolcanoRSS.xml
markets.YES_condition: At least one volcano appears in the 'New Activity' (not 'Ongoing Activity') section of any Smithsonian/USGS Weekly Volcanic Activity Report bulletin published with bulletin date between 2026-04-22 and 2026-05-13 inclusive.
markets.why_interesting: The 'New Activity' list typically gets one or two volcanoes per week worldwide. A 3-week window gives this strong YES odds, but it's not certain.
markets.ui_description: The Smithsonian's Weekly Volcanic Activity Report is the gold standard for tracking eruption onset, jointly published with USGS each Wednesday. The 'New Activity' section flags volcanoes whose eruption status changed in the past week — typically 1-3 per bulletin worldwide. With three weekly bulletins in the window, this market combines baseline probability with weekly liquidity events.

markets.title: NASA FIRMS detects an active wildfire in continental US >1000 hectares during April 22 – May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: NASA FIRMS active fire data (VIIRS S-NPP and NOAA-20) cross-referenced with InciWeb (inciweb.wildfire.gov) for confirmed incident size.
markets.YES_condition: InciWeb lists at least one active wildfire incident in the contiguous United States with reported size ≥1000 hectares (≈2,470 acres) and ignition date between 2026-04-22 and 2026-05-11 inclusive.
markets.why_interesting: Late April / early May is the start of southwest US fire season. Drought conditions and Santa Ana / Diablo wind events make this a function of climate and weather.
markets.ui_description: The American West fire season is starting earlier each year, with Southwest grass fires and Florida muck fires both possible in late April. NASA FIRMS provides satellite hotspot detection within 3 hours of pass; InciWeb gives the official incident size. Traders watch drought monitor maps, fuel moisture content, and forecast wind events.

markets.title: A named tropical cyclone forms in any basin worldwide during April 22 – May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: NOAA National Hurricane Center, Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC), and regional tropical cyclone warning centers (RSMCs).
markets.YES_condition: Any official RSMC (NHC, JTWC, JMA, RSMC La Réunion, BoM, FMS, or RSMC Nadi) assigns a name to a tropical cyclone with formation date between 2026-04-22 and 2026-05-11 inclusive.
markets.why_interesting: April-May is off-season for the Atlantic but peak for the Indian Ocean post-monsoon, plus West Pacific can fire any time. A 20-day window globally is a near-certain YES.
markets.ui_description: Late April and early May straddle the end of Southern Hemisphere cyclone season and the start of the West Pacific season. Atlantic and East Pacific are mostly quiet, but Indian Ocean basins can produce a system in 24 hours. Globally a named storm forms roughly once every 5 days, making this market a near-certain YES with most trading in the spread.

markets.title: Iceland Reykjanes peninsula records ≥50 M1.5+ earthquakes in any 24-hour period during April 22 – May 11, 2026
markets.resolution_source: Icelandic Meteorological Office (Veðurstofa Íslands) earthquake catalog at en.vedur.is/earthquakes-and-volcanism, region 'Reykjanes Peninsula'.
markets.YES_condition: Within any rolling 24-hour window starting between 2026-04-22 00:00 UTC and 2026-05-11 00:00 UTC, the IMO catalog lists ≥50 earthquakes with mag ≥1.5 located on the Reykjanes peninsula (geographic bounding box 63.7-64.1°N, -22.9 to -22.0°E).
markets.why_interesting: Reykjanes has been in an eruptive phase since 2021. Magma intrusions trigger swarms of hundreds of quakes — every Icelander watches IMO charts the way Americans watch weather radar.
markets.ui_description: The Reykjanes peninsula has hosted nine eruptions since March 2021, each preceded by a sharp earthquake swarm as magma intrudes into shallow crust. The Icelandic Met Office publishes raw catalog data with single-minute latency, and 50 M1.5+ events in 24 hours is the rough threshold where Civil Protection raises alert level. A market that prices the next eruption's geophysical fingerprint.
